description: This figure presents SEM fractography of tensile-fractured Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot-rolling reductions: (a) HR45 (45% reduction), (b) HR68 (68% reduction), and (c) HR82 (82% reduction). All micrographs, captured at a scale of 20 μm, reveal typical ductile fracture morphology characterized by numerous dimples (circled) and microvoids (indicated by arrows). The fracture surfaces exhibit a mixture of fine equiaxed dimples and larger microvoids nucleated at (Nb,Mo,W)C particles. Notably, the dimple size in HR45 is visibly larger than in HR68 and HR82, correlating with its larger mean grain size (21.82 μm vs. 11.55 μm and 7.02 μm). The microvoids form via debonding of intragranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles, alleviating strain concentration at intergranular carbide interfaces. Despite increasing rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, the ductile fracture characteristic is retained with abundant dimples observed across all samples, indicating that toughness remains largely unaffected. This demonstrates that the intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles, while acting as preferential nucleation sites for microcracks, do not detrimentally impact toughness due to stress relief through microvoid formation.
